request.question: What is the name of the regional transportation authority that includes the station which is proximal to Cira Centre South?

title: Tampa Bay Area Regional Transportation Authority
South Florida Regional Transportation Authority
Santa Ana Regional Transportation Center
30th Street Station
Berks Area Regional Transportation Authority
Anaheim Regional Transportation Intermodal Center
Franklin Street station (Pennsylvania)
William F. Walsh Regional Transportation Center
Tri-Rail
Cira Centre South
sentences: The Tampa Bay Area Regional Transportation Authority, or TBARTA, is a regional transportation agency of the U.S. state of Florida which was created on July 1, 2007.

The purpose of the agency is "to plan, develop, finance, construct, own, purchase, operate, maintain, relocate, equip, repair, and manage multimodal systems in Citrus, Hernando, Hillsborough, Manatee, Pasco, Pinellas, and Sarasota Counties."
The agency coordinates its efforts with the Florida Department of Transportation to improve transportation in the Tampa Bay Area.
The South Florida Regional Transportation Authority (SFRTA) is a tri-county public transit authority operating out of Pompano Beach, Florida, serving Miami-Dade, Broward and Palm Beach counties.
The organization was created on July 1, 2003 by the Florida Legislature and enacted by the Florida Department of Transportation.

Replacing the pre-existing Tri-County Commuter Rail Authority, the goal of incorporation was to expand cooperation between the Tri-Rail commuter rail service and the existing county public transport authorities: Broward County Transit, Miami-Dade Transit, and Palm Tran.
Tri-Rail is solely operated by SFRTA.
Santa Ana Regional Transportation Center is a passenger rail station and transportation center in Santa Ana, California.
It is used by Amtrak's "Pacific Surfliner" and Metrolink's Orange County Line and Inland Empire-Orange County Line trains.
It is also a Greyhound station and a hub for the Orange County Transportation Authority bus system as well as a terminal for several Mexican bus tour companies.

The 30th Street Station is the main railroad station in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, United States and one of the seven stations in Southeastern Pennsylvania Transportation Authority's (SEPTA) Center City fare zone.
It is also a major stop on Amtrak's (National Railroad Passenger Corporation) Northeast and Keystone Corridors.

Berks Area Regional Transportation Authority (BARTA), previously Berks Area Reading Transportation Authority, is a public transportation system serving the city of Reading and its surrounding area of Berks County, Pennsylvania.
Dennis D. Louwerse, the long-time executive director/CEO at BARTA, died on Thursday, September 5, 2013 at the age of 68.
He became the executive director at BARTA in 1983, and he was the executive director for 30 years until his death in 2013.
David W. Kilmer is currently the executive director at BARTA and Red Rose Transit Authority.
The Anaheim Regional Transportation Intermodal Center (ARTIC) is an intermodal transit center in Anaheim, California, United States.

It serves as a train station for Amtrak intercity rail and Metrolink commuter rail, as well as a bus station used by the Orange County Transportation Authority (OCTA), Anaheim Resort Transportation (ART), Greyhound, Megabus, and Tres Estrellas de Oro.
Franklin Street station is a bus station and former railroad station in Reading, Pennsylvania.
It currently serves Berks Area Regional Transportation Authority (BARTA) buses and hosts a transportation museum.
The William F. Walsh Regional Transportation Center is the long-distance ground travel (rail and bus) terminal serving the Syracuse, New York area.
It is served by Amtrak, Greyhound Lines, Megabus, and Trailways.
Local and regional bus transportation is provided by the Central New York Regional Transportation Authority (CENTRO).
Various taxi firms service the Center, as well.
The Regional Transportation Center is located on the north side of Syracuse, adjacent to the Central New York Regional Market and near Destiny USA.
Tri-Rail (reporting mark TRCX) is a commuter rail line linking Miami, Fort Lauderdale, and West Palm Beach, Florida, United States.
It is managed by the South Florida Regional Transportation Authority (SFRTA) along CSX Transportation's former Miami Subdivision, the line now wholly owned by the Florida DOT.

The 70.9 mi system has 18 stations along the Southeast Florida coast, and connects directly to Amtrak at numerous stations, and to Metrorail at the Tri-Rail and Metrorail Transfer Station and at Miami Airport station.
Cira Centre South is a complex of two skyscrapers in the University City district of Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, which is across the Schuylkill River from Center City Philadelphia.
The location of the buildings is between Walnut Street and Chestnut Street south of 30th Street Station and the Old Post Office Building.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- If the `passages` consistently corroborate the context claim and are identity-aligned, append one brief paraphrased supporting sentence that cites the corroboration (keep the summary compact).
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: What is the name of the regional transportation authority that includes the station which is proximal to Cira Centre South?
hop1_docs: Cira Centre South | Cira Centre South is a complex of two skyscrapers in the University City district of Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, which is across the Schuylkill River from Center City Philadelphia.  The location of the buildings is between Walnut Street and Chestnut Street south of 30th Street Station and the Old Post Office Building.

Cira Centre | The Cira Centre is a 29-story, 437 ft office high-rise in the University City section of Philadelphia, across the street from Amtrak's 30th Street Station.  Developed by Brandywine Realty Trust and designed by César Pelli, it was built in 2004-05 on a platform over rail tracks.

Transportation in Boston | The Boston transportation system includes roadway, subway, regional rail, air, and sea options for passenger and freight transit in Boston, Massachusetts.  The Massachusetts Port Authority (Massport) operates the Port of Boston, which includes a container shipping facility in South Boston, and Logan International Airport, in East Boston.  The Massachusetts Bay Transportation Authority (MBTA) operates bus, subway, short distance rail, and water ferry passenger services throughout the city and region.  Amtrak operates passenger rail service to and from major northeastern cities.  A major bus terminal at South Station is served by varied intercity bus companies.  The city is bisected by major highways I-90 and I-93, the intersection of which has undergone a major renovation, nicknamed the Big Dig.

Canadian Internet Registration Authority | The Canadian Internet Registration Authority (CIRA) (French: Autorité canadienne pour les enregistrements Internet ACEI) is the organization that manages the . CA country code top-level domain, the policies that support Canada’s Internet community and Canada’s involvement in international Internet governance.  CIRA is a member-driven organization.  Membership in CIRA is included in the cost of a . CA domain, and open to anyone who holds a . CA domain.  There are approximately 15,000 CIRA members as of January 2013.

Metrolinx | Metrolinx is a Crown agency that manages and integrates road and public transport in the Golden Horseshoe region, which includes the cities of Toronto and Hamilton, in the province of Ontario in Canada.  Headquartered in Union Station in Toronto, the organization was created by the Government of Ontario as the "Greater Toronto Transportation Authority" on April 24, 2006.  It adopted the public name, Metrolinx, in 2007.

Transit-proximate development | Transit-proximate development is a term used by some planning officials to describe (potentially dense) development that is physically near a public transport node (e.g. a bus station, train station or metro station).  This type of development includes transit-oriented development, but, according to some planning officials, can also describe development that is not transit-oriented development.  Thus, transit-proximate development can include results where, despite the location of dense development near transit, the development does not take full advantage of -- or fully encourage the use of -- the public transport node.  For example, transit-proximate development could include buildings with extensive parking facilities typical of suburban locations, a lack of "mixed-use development" (housing, workplaces and shopping in the same place), or a lack of extensive pedestrian facilities that would make it easier for people to reach the public transport node.

Glenanda | Glenanda is a suburb of Johannesburg, South Africa.  It is located in Region F.  Glenanda borders the suburbs of Glenvista, Suideroord and Winchester Hills.  Glenanda has its own public library, service station and local shopping centre.  A primary school is established in Glenanda which services the surrounding areas.  Glenanda is in close proximity to The Glen Shopping Centre with an average travelling time of five minutes.
response.summary_1: Passage [1] locates Cira Centre South "south of 30th Street Station" (bridge entity = 30th Street Station). Missing fact: the name of the regional transportation authority that includes/operates 30th Street Station. Next hop: retrieve the 30th Street Station page or authoritative regional transit agency (SEPTA/Amtrak) sources and look for "30th Street Station" + "operated by"/"part of" to confirm the regional authority.
response.hop2_query: 30th Street Station operated by SEPTA Amtrak
hop2_docs: 30th Street Station | The 30th Street Station is the main railroad station in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, United States and one of the seven stations in Southeastern Pennsylvania Transportation Authority's (SEPTA) Center City fare zone.  It is also a major stop on Amtrak's (National Railroad Passenger Corporation) Northeast and Keystone Corridors.

Torresdale station | Torresdale station is a SEPTA Regional Rail station in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.  Located at Grant Avenue and James Street in the Torresdale neighborhood of Northeast Philadelphia, it serves the Trenton Line.  It is 14.8 miles from 30th Street Station.  In 2004 this station saw 863 boardings on an average weekday.  Though located on Amtrak's Northeast Corridor, Amtrak does not stop at this station.  Torresdale has a 331-space parking lot.

30th Street station (subway) | 30th Street station is an underground SEPTA rapid transit and trolley station in Philadelphia.  It serves the Market–Frankford Line and SEPTA Subway–Surface Trolley Lines.  It is located on Market Street between 30th and 31st Streets in the University City neighborhood near the main 30th Street Station and Drexel University.  The station features four tracks—two outer tracks for the Subway-Surface trolleys, and two inner tracks for the Market-Frankford subway trains.  A free transfer is available between both services via a mezzanine level which provides access to all four tracks.

Highland Avenue station (SEPTA) | Highland Avenue station is a station along the SEPTA Wilmington/Newark Line and Amtrak's Northeast Corridor, one of two stations in Chester, Pennsylvania, United States.  Amtrak trains do not stop there; it is only served by SEPTA.  The line offers southbound service to Wilmington and Newark, Delaware and northbound service to Philadelphia.  The station is located on Highland Avenue (US 13) and 6th Street in Chester, PA.

Lancaster and Lansdowne station | Lancaster and Lansdowne station is a SEPTA Subway-Surface Lines trolley station in Philadelphia located in West Philadelphia.  The former Amtrak and SEPTA Regional Rail station at 52nd Street can be seen from this station.

22nd Street station (SEPTA) | 22nd Street station is a subway station in Philadelphia.  It is located in Center City and serves the SEPTA Subway–Surface Trolley Lines.  Similar to 19th Street station, it has two side platforms with a total of four tracks.  Only the two outer tracks serve the station; the two inner tracks are used for the Market-Frankford Line as it travels between 15th Street and 30th Street station.  Since the station is only served by the trolley lines, there is no fare collection at the station; all fares are paid on board the trolley itself.  This will change with the SEPTA Key system, as turnstiles will be installed at this station.

Croydon station (SEPTA) | Croydon station is a SEPTA Regional Rail station in Croydon, Pennsylvania.  It serves the Trenton Line.  It is located at Bristol Pike and Cedar Avenue, and is 19.9 miles from 30th Street Station.
response.summary_2: The regional transportation authority is the Southeastern Pennsylvania Transportation Authority (SEPTA); Passage [1] identifies 30th Street Station as one of the seven stations in SEPTA's Center City fare zone.
